meta.name: Ghanaian Shito (Black Pepper Sauce)
meta.subtitle: Deep, smoky, spicy condiment—perfect with waakye, kenkey, rice, eggs, and fried fish.

source.name: SSA Canonical Catalog
notes: This is a canonical, shelf-stable-leaning shito built for SSA catalogs.
Heat level is adjustable by chili type/amount; use dried shrimp for classic flavor or omit for vegan.
Oil acts as a preservative barrier; keep utensils dry/clean to extend fridge life.

summary: Shito is a Ghanaian black pepper sauce made by slowly frying aromatics and dried seafood (or mushrooms for vegan) with spices until dark and intensely flavorful.

yield.notes: Makes ~2 to 2.5 cups depending on evaporation. Use 1–2 tbsp per serving.

optional.label: Dehydrator (optional)
optional.notes: Useful if you want to pre-dry onions/peppers for even longer shelf life.

notes: Contains dried shrimp and optionally smoked fish—shellfish/fish allergens.
For vegan version, omit shrimp/fish and use mushrooms + extra umami seasoning.

ingredients.label: Neutral oil (vegetable/canola/peanut)
notes: Oil is essential for texture and preservation. You can reduce slightly, but shelf life may decrease.
Peanut oil is traditional in some homes; neutral oil keeps flavor balanced.

ingredients.label: Habanero or Scotch bonnet (fresh), stemmed
notes: Heat varies by pepper size and variety. Start with 2 for medium heat, 4–6 for hot.
For more consistent results, weigh peppers and adjust next batch.

ingredients.label: Dried chili flakes or dried ground chili
notes: This drives the deep red heat that darkens during frying.
If using very hot flakes, reduce to 10–15 g.

ingredients.label: Dried shrimp (ground or to be ground)
notes: Classic shito uses dried shrimp; rinse quickly and pat dry if very salty or sandy.
If already ground, you can add later to reduce splatter.

ingredients.label: Smoked fish powder (or flaked smoked fish, well-dried)
notes: Optional but adds depth. Use very dry fish to prevent spoilage.
Skip if you want shrimp-only shito.

ingredients.label: Ground black pepper
ingredients.notes: Shito is often called black pepper sauce—pepper should be noticeable.

ingredients.label: Ground cloves (tiny pinch) or anise (optional)
ingredients.notes: Use sparingly—this is a background warmth, not a dominant note.

ingredients.label: Koobi (salted dried fish) powder or shrimp seasoning (optional)
notes: Only if you have it—this can get salty fast.
If using, reduce added salt and taste carefully.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt
notes: Adjust based on dried shrimp/fish saltiness and any seasoning powders.
Aim slightly under-salted; shito concentrates as it cools.

ingredients.label: MSG or umami seasoning (optional)
ingredients.notes: Optional—use if you want extra savory punch.

notes: Splatter risk: keep heat moderate and stir frequently. A splatter screen helps.
The goal is slow frying until the mixture darkens and oil separates clearly on top.
For best keeping quality: jars must be clean/dry; always use a clean, dry spoon.

changes.remove: Omit dried shrimp and smoked fish.
changes.add: Add 20 g dried mushrooms (powdered) and/or 1–2 tsp umami seasoning.
changes.salt: Taste and adjust; often needs a little more salt/umami to compensate.

changes.add: Add 1 tsp smoked paprika and use smoked fish powder.
changes.note: Keep smoke subtle—shito should stay balanced.

changes.heat: Use 2 fresh habaneros and reduce dried chili to 10–12 g.
changes.balance: Consider adding the optional brown sugar to soften bitterness.

notes: Shito involves long frying in oil—use caution with hot oil and splatter.
Ensure seafood components are very dry; moisture increases spoilage risk.
If you see mold, off smells, or bubbling/fermentation, discard.

fridge.duration: up to 4 weeks (often longer with good hygiene)
notes: Store in clean jar. Keep a thin layer of oil covering the surface.
Always use a clean, dry spoon to avoid introducing water.

freezer.notes: Freeze in small containers. Thaw in fridge; stir to recombine.

steps.title: Prep and blend aromatics
instructions: Roughly chop onion. Peel ginger and garlic.
Add onion, ginger, garlic, and fresh habaneros to a blender/processor.
Blend to a coarse paste. If needed, add 1–2 tbsp oil (not water) to help it move.
checks: Mixture is a thick paste (not watery).

steps.title: Optional: prepare dried shrimp and smoked fish
instructions: If using whole dried shrimp, quickly inspect and remove any grit.
Optionally rinse very briefly and pat completely dry (avoid adding moisture).
Grind shrimp to a coarse powder. If using smoked fish, ensure it is very dry and powder/flakes are ready.
checks: Seafood components are dry and ready to add.

steps.title: Start the fry: cook down the paste
instructions: Heat oil in a heavy pot over medium heat.
Carefully add the blended paste (it will splatter). Stir immediately.
Cook 10–12 minutes, stirring often, until moisture reduces and the paste thickens and darkens slightly.
checks: Paste has reduced; splatter is less violent.
No burning on the bottom—scrape and stir.

steps.title: Bloom spices and build the base
instructions: Stir in dried chili flakes/ground chili and black pepper.
Add bay leaves if using.
Cook 2–3 minutes, stirring constantly, to bloom spices in the oil.
checks: Spices smell toasted and fragrant, not burnt.

steps.title: Add seafood umami (or vegan umami)
instructions: If using dried shrimp powder, stir it in and cook 2 minutes.
If using smoked fish powder/flakes, add now and stir to combine.
Optional: add koobi/shrimp seasoning and/or MSG/umami seasoning (use sparingly).
checks: Mixture is cohesive and evenly mixed.

steps.title: Slow-fry until dark and oil separates
instructions: Reduce heat to low (a gentle, steady sizzle).
Slow-fry 40–60 minutes, stirring every 3–5 minutes and scraping the bottom.
If the mixture threatens to burn, lower heat and stir more often.
Shito is ready when it is very dark, aromatic, and oil clearly pools on top and around the edges.

steps.title: Season and finish
instructions: Taste carefully (it’s hot). Add salt gradually to balance.
Optional: add brown sugar if you want to soften bitterness and round the edges.
Remove bay leaves if used.
Let shito cool 5–10 minutes so solids settle and oil rises.
checks: Flavor is balanced: spicy, smoky, savory, with a rounded finish.

steps.title: Jar and store safely
instructions: Spoon shito into clean, dry jars.
Ensure a thin layer of oil covers the surface (top up with a little oil if needed).
Cool completely, cap, and refrigerate.
checks: Jar rims are clean; surface is covered with oil; lids are tight.
